@graph.@id: https://pinnacleblooms.org/intervention-techniques/augmentative and alternative communication / expressive language / functional communication/aac requesting skills / manding / functional communication training / early aac use/9-materials-that-help-aac-for-requesting#faq

mainEntity.name: Why does we got my son an aac device six months ago. a good one — one of the apps everyone recommends, with hundreds of words organized into categories, core vocabulary on the main page, the whole system designed by experts. his speech therapist helped us set it up, customized it with his favorite things, showed us how it worked. i was so hopeful. i imagined him touching symbols to tell me what he wanted, finally ending the guessing games that dominate our days. no more pointing at the pantry while i name food after food, watching his frustration build. no more meltdowns because i opened the wrong drawer or offered the wrong cup. finally, he would have words. but six months later, the device mostly sits on the kitchen counter, screen dark, gathering dust next to the mail pile. he'll look at it sometimes when i bring it over. he'll touch random buttons if we put it in his hands, watching the app speak words that have nothing to do with what he wants. sometimes he presses the same button over and over just to hear it talk. but he doesn't use it to ask for things. not for his favorite snack — the one he'll melt down for, the one he clearly wants desperately. not for the toy on the high shelf that he stares at and reaches toward. not for more tickles when he's laughing and clearly wants them to continue. not to go outside, though he stands at the door and whines. not for anything at all. he still reaches and pulls my hand toward what he wants. he still takes me to the refrigerator and pushes my hand toward the handle. he still whines and melts down when i guess wrong three times in a row. the device is right there, inches away, with all the words he needs, and he doesn't use it. i've started to wonder if he can't learn to use it. if maybe the device was a mistake. if we're asking something of him that he's not capable of. his therapist says that's not it — she says we need to teach him to request. that having the vocabulary isn't the same as knowing how to use it. that the device is the tool, but requesting is the skill, and skills need to be taught. that makes sense when she explains it, but i don't know how to do it at home. how do i teach him that touching the 'cookie' symbol on a screen means he gets a cookie? how do i help him understand that the picture represents the thing? how do i bridge the gap between what he wants in his head and the symbol that could express it? i've tried pointing to the symbol and saying 'cookie' over and over. i've tried hand-over-hand, guiding his finger to the screen. but it feels forced and mechanical, and he pulls away, resists, sometimes melts down before we even start. i've tried waiting — putting the device out and waiting for him to figure it out — but he just gets frustrated and goes back to pulling my hand. there has to be a better way. he wants things constantly — all day long, he wants. he has motivation in abundance. he has the device with all the words. something in between isn't connecting. i see other kids using aac to ask for things, making choices, building sentences. i don't know why he can't get there. i don't know what we're doing wrong?

acceptedAnswer.text: AAC REQUESTING SKILLS / MANDING WITH AAC / FUNCTIONAL COMMUNICATION TRAINING / EARLY AAC SKILL DEVELOPMENT! Requesting (also called 'manding' in behavioral terms) is one of the earliest and most fundamental communication functions — using communication to get something you want or need. For AAC users, requesting involves learning that activating a symbol (touching, pointing, selecting) results in receiving the corresponding item, action, or attention. This requires understanding symbol-referent relationships (the picture means the thing), motor planning to access the symbol, initiation (using AAC without prompting), and generalization across contexts. Many children who have AAC available do not automatically use it for requesting — this skill must be explicitly taught through systematic instruction that pairs high motivation, clear contingencies (request → receive), and appropriate prompting and fading strategies. Successful requesting builds the foundation for all other communication functions.

mainEntity.name: What is AAC Requesting Skills / Manding with AAC / Functional Communication Training / Early AAC Skill Development?

acceptedAnswer.text: Requesting (also called 'manding' in behavioral terms) is one of the earliest and most fundamental communication functions — using communication to get something you want or need. For AAC users, requesting involves learning that activating a symbol (touching, pointing, selecting) results in receiving the corresponding item, action, or attention. This requires understanding symbol-referent relationships (the picture means the thing), motor planning to access the symbol, initiation (using AAC without prompting), and generalization across contexts. Many children who have AAC available do not automatically use it for requesting — this skill must be explicitly taught through systematic instruction that pairs high motivation, clear contingencies (request → receive), and appropriate prompting and fading strategies. Successful requesting builds the foundation for all other communication functions. Common signs include: Has AAC device or system but doesn't use it to ask for things, Continues to use pre-AAC behaviors for requesting (reaching, leading, crying), Touches AAC symbols randomly without clear intent, Only uses AAC when heavily prompted or hand-over-hand guided, Doesn't initiate communication with AAC independently.

mainEntity.name: How does a High-Motivation Item Sets with Matching Symbols help with aac requesting skills?

acceptedAnswer.text: Requesting is driven by motivation — children request things they want. Teaching requesting with low-interest items creates an uphill battle. High-motivation item sets ensure the child has genuine desire to communicate. These sets pair actual motivating items (preferred snacks, favorite toys, beloved activities) with their corresponding AAC symbols. The key is identifying what the specific child truly wants — not what we think they should want, but what they actually reach for, melt down over, light up about. When the motivation is high enough, the child has a reason to learn the communication route to get it. Materials include preferred item assessments, symbol-matched item containers, and motivation inventories. The principle is simple: start with what they desperately want, then expand from there. Price: ₹200–1,500. DIY option: Create a motivation inventory: observe what your child reaches for, requests non-verbally, melts down without. Print or find symbols for each high-motivation item. Keep these items and symbols together, ready for requesting opportunities. Safety: Never withhold necessary items (water, meals, comfort) to teach requesting. Use highly preferred extras and treats, not basic needs.

mainEntity.name: How does a Requesting Communication Boards (Core + High-Frequency Wants) help with aac requesting skills?

acceptedAnswer.text: Full AAC systems with hundreds of words can be overwhelming for early requesters. Requesting-focused boards provide a simplified, targeted layout combining core vocabulary (want, more, help, all done, that, please, go) with high-frequency desired items and activities. This reduces cognitive load and motor demand while maintaining language-building potential. Core words like 'want' and 'more' combine with specific items: 'want cookie,' 'more swing,' 'want that.' The board stays visible and accessible during natural requesting moments throughout the day. For children just learning that symbols get them things, a requesting-focused board builds success before expanding to full vocabulary. The layout should reflect the child's actual wants — not generic clipart, but their snacks, their toys, their people. Price: ₹200–1,000. DIY option: Create a board with core requesting words (want, more, help, all done, please, go, stop, that) plus 8-12 of the child's most wanted items and activities. Laminate. Keep it within reach during routines where requesting happens naturally. Safety: The board should be available, not controlled. The child should be able to see and access it when they want things.

mainEntity.name: How does a Symbol-to-Object Matching Materials help with aac requesting skills?

acceptedAnswer.text: Before a child can use symbols to request, they need to understand that symbols represent real things — that the picture of a cookie means an actual cookie. Symbol-to-object matching materials build this foundational understanding through direct pairing. Activities include matching symbol cards to real objects, sorting symbols with corresponding items, and receptive identification (give me the symbol that matches this cracker). This work strengthens the symbol-referent connection that makes symbolic communication possible. Some children grasp this intuitively; others need explicit teaching. Matching materials make the abstract concrete: this picture IS that thing. Once the connection is solid, the symbol becomes a tool for getting the thing. Price: ₹300–1,500. DIY option: Take photos of the child's actual items (their snacks, their toys). Print and laminate. Practice matching: put the real item out and have the child find its symbol. Gradually, the symbol becomes the route to the item. Safety: Keep matching activities playful and successful. If frustration builds, simplify — fewer items, clearer differences, more support.

mainEntity.name: How does a Interrupted Activity Materials help with aac requesting skills?

acceptedAnswer.text: Motivation for requesting peaks when something good stops unexpectedly. Interrupted activity materials create systematic opportunities to request 'more' or continuation. Cause-and-effect toys that stop (bubble machines, spinning toys, wind-up toys), activities with natural pauses (swinging, tickling, music), and controllable items (videos that pause, toys with on/off access) all create moments where the child wants continuation and can learn that requesting makes it happen. The sequence is powerful: child enjoys activity → activity stops → child experiences wanting more → adult models or prompts 'more' on AAC → activity resumes immediately. The contingency is clear: request = continuation. Interrupted activities harness existing motivation and create natural, repetitive requesting opportunities throughout the day. Price: ₹300–2,000. DIY option: Identify activities your child loves that can be easily paused: bubbles, swinging, tickles, favorite songs, spinning toys. Keep the 'more' symbol visible. Stop mid-activity, wait, model or prompt 'more,' and immediately continue when they request. Safety: Don't let the child become too frustrated before offering the communication opportunity. The pause should be brief — create the want, provide the solution.

mainEntity.name: How does a Errorless Teaching and Prompt Fading Materials help with aac requesting skills?

acceptedAnswer.text: Many children with AAC have experienced communication failure repeatedly — attempts that weren't understood, needs that went unmet, frustration that led nowhere. Errorless teaching materials ensure the child experiences success from the first requesting attempt, building confidence and clear contingencies before fading support. Materials include prompt hierarchy guides, visual prompting supports, and fading schedules. The approach: provide enough support that the child succeeds (full physical guidance if needed), then systematically reduce prompts over time. The child never has to 'fail' to learn — they experience success, then gradually do more independently. This prevents prompt dependency when fading is planned from the start and prevents the learned helplessness that comes from repeated failure. Price: ₹200–1,000. DIY option: Learn the prompt hierarchy: full physical (hand-over-hand) → partial physical (guiding the arm) → gestural (pointing toward the symbol) → visual (looking at the symbol) → independent. Start where the child succeeds. Fade one level at a time. Document what level of prompt is currently needed. Safety: Hand-over-hand prompting should be done gently and sensitively. If the child resists physical guidance, try less intrusive prompts first or work with a therapist on alternatives.

mainEntity.name: How does a Aided Language Modeling Scripts and Cue Cards help with aac requesting skills?

acceptedAnswer.text: Children learn to request by seeing requesting modeled — watching others use AAC to ask for things. Modeling scripts and cue cards help communication partners consistently demonstrate requesting throughout daily routines. Scripts provide specific language: during snack, point to 'want' and 'cracker' while saying 'I want cracker' before giving the child crackers. During play, model 'more blocks' while requesting blocks. Cue cards posted around the house remind partners to model during natural requesting moments. The child sees that symbols are how we ask for things — not just something they're expected to do, but something everyone does. Modeling scripts reduce the cognitive load on adults, making consistent modeling sustainable across partners and environments. Price: ₹100–500. DIY option: Create cue cards for each high-frequency routine: snack time ('I want [item], more [item], all done'), playtime ('want [toy], more [activity], help please'), outside time ('go outside, want swing, more push'). Post them where routines happen. Practice modeling before asking the child to request. Safety: Model more than you prompt. Children should see significantly more demonstrations of AAC requesting than demands to use it themselves.

citation.name: Research on AAC and Functional Communication
citation.description: Research demonstrates that systematic requesting instruction using evidence-based strategies significantly improves AAC use for functional communication. Key principles include: high motivation pairing, aided language modeling, systematic prompting and fading, natural environment training, and ensuring the request-reinforcement contingency is clear and consistent. Having access to vocabulary is necessary but not sufficient; explicit teaching of requesting as a communication function is essential for many AAC users.

The approach: provide enough support that the child succeeds (full physical guidance if needed), then systematically reduce prompts over time. The child never has to 'fail' to learn — they experience success, then gradually do more independently. This prevents prompt dependency when fading is planned from the start and prevents the learned helplessness that comes from repeated failure.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Learn the prompt hierarchy: full physical (hand-over-hand) → partial physical (guiding the arm) → gestural (pointing toward the symbol) → visual (looking at the symbol) → independent. Start where the child succeeds. Fade one level at a time. Document what level of prompt is currently needed.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Hand-over-hand prompting should be done gently and sensitively. If the child resists physical guidance, try less intrusive prompts first or work with a therapist on alternatives.

item.name: Aided Language Modeling Scripts and Cue Cards
item.description: Children learn to request by seeing requesting modeled — watching others use AAC to ask for things. Modeling scripts and cue cards help communication partners consistently demonstrate requesting throughout daily routines. Scripts provide specific language: during snack, point to 'want' and 'cracker' while saying 'I want cracker' before giving the child crackers. During play, model 'more blocks' while requesting blocks. Cue cards posted around the house remind partners to model during natural requesting moments. The child sees that symbols are how we ask for things — not just something they're expected to do, but something everyone does. Modeling scripts reduce the cognitive load on adults, making consistent modeling sustainable across partners and environments.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create cue cards for each high-frequency routine: snack time ('I want [item], more [item], all done'), playtime ('want [toy], more [activity], help please'), outside time ('go outside, want swing, more push'). Post them where routines happen. Practice modeling before asking the child to request.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Model more than you prompt. Children should see significantly more demonstrations of AAC requesting than demands to use it themselves.

item.name: Wait Time and Communication Temptation Materials
item.description: Many adults jump in too quickly — providing what the child wants before they have a chance to request, or prompting so fast that independent initiation never develops. Wait time materials and communication temptation setups create space for the child to initiate. Temptations include: desired items in view but out of reach, containers the child can't open, activities that require adult help to start, incomplete tasks (snack with no drink, puzzle with missing piece). The setup creates the want; the wait time allows the child to express it. Visual timers can help adults wait long enough (10-15 seconds feels very long but is often necessary). The principle: create the motivation, make AAC accessible, and wait. Many children will initiate if given the space.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Set up temptations: favorite snacks in clear containers the child can't open, toys on visible but unreachable shelves, activities that need you to start. Keep AAC visible. When the child wants something, wait 10-15 seconds before jumping in. Give them space to initiate.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Wait time should not become withholding until frustration. If the child becomes distressed, provide support. The goal is initiation opportunity, not frustration tolerance training.

item.name: Natural Environment Teaching Materials
item.description: Requesting skills learned only in structured therapy sessions often don't transfer to real life. Natural environment teaching (NET) materials bring requesting practice into everyday moments where communication naturally happens — meals, play, transitions, outings. Materials include portable AAC setups for different environments, routine-embedded requesting opportunities, and generalization planning guides. The kitchen during snack prep becomes requesting practice. The playground becomes 'help push' and 'more swing' practice. The car becomes 'want music' practice. When requesting is practiced where life happens, the child learns that AAC works everywhere, with everyone, for real wants — not just in therapy rooms for pretend motivators. Generalization is built in, not hoped for.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Identify 5-10 environments where requesting naturally happens. Create portable or posted AAC access for each (mini-board in the car, requesting board in the kitchen, symbol strip on the playground fence). Practice requesting in each environment with real motivators.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: AAC should be available in ALL environments, not just practice ones. The child's voice goes everywhere.

item.name: Choice-Making Materials with Immediate Reinforcement
item.description: Choice-making is requesting in its simplest form: you show me two things, I pick one, I get it. Choice-making materials with immediate reinforcement build the request-receive contingency in the clearest possible way. Materials include choice boards with two options, choice containers with visible items, and structured choice routines where selection always leads to receiving. The child learns: when I indicate this one, I get this one. That understanding becomes the foundation for all requesting — the clear contingency that makes communication worthwhile. For children who don't yet use symbols, choices can start with actual objects, then transition to photos, then to symbols. The immediate reinforcement (choosing = getting) builds the understanding that communication works.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Start every snack, activity, and transition with a choice. Two options, held up or displayed. The child indicates (reaching, pointing, eye gaze). Immediately give what was chosen. Be consistent — always honor the choice. Transition from objects to photos to symbols as understanding grows.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Always honor the choice. If the child picks something and you don't give it, trust in communication breaks down. Only offer choices you'll honor.

socialMedia.caption: The words are there. The wanting is there. Something in between isn't connecting. When your child has AAC but isn't using it to ask for things, these materials bridge the gap: high-motivation item sets, requesting boards, symbol-object matching, interrupted activities, errorless teaching, modeling scripts, wait time setups, natural environment materials, and choice-making with immediate reinforcement. Requesting is a skill that needs teaching — and once it clicks, communication makes sense. Save for AAC success! 🔖 #AACRequesting #TeachingAAC #PinnacleBlooms
